text: The surgeon identifies a large endometrioma on the left side, noting its close proximity to another structure. As the surgeon separates the ovarian endometrioma, the invasion of the right endometrioma into the left is visible. Multiple cystic structures are observed in the left ovary, indicative of polycystic ovarian syndrome.
The surgeon trims the right ovary, which appears severely damaged with paper-thin cortical tissue and no visible follicles. The lining is carefully pulled out and removed.

The surgeon peels away the thin cortical tissue, resembling the layers of an onion, revealing minimal follicular presence. The lining of a larger cyst is stripped off, exposing another cyst within, which is then opened.
The surgeon removes a cyst, peeling off its lining, revealing chocolate-colored material indicative of endometrioma content.

The surgeon examines the left ovary, which displays multiple follicles indicative of polycystic ovarian condition. There is visible invasion from the right ovary to the left ovary. The ovary shows multiloculation, a typical feature of polycystic ovary, and the surgeon attempts to remove the lining using traction and counter-traction techniques.

The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical plane to avoid damaging surrounding structures. A small follicle is visible, releasing chocolate-colored material. The surgeon removes the yellow lining of the cyst, revealing a larger cyst with multiple loculations. The lining of the cyst is extracted, and the remaining ovarian tissue appears abnormal with reduced volume. Several particles are visible at the bottom of the field.

The surgeon examines the pelvic cavity, focusing on a subserosal broad-based myoma. The myoma's capsule, previously invaded by endometrioma, is being carefully pulled out, revealing its structure as it is extracted.

The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to pull out the myoma from the pelvic cavity, revealing a wide surgical bed with some oozing but no active bleeding. The vascular pedicle is trimmed, and the base of the myoma is closed with interrupted extracorporeal sutures. The pelvic cavity is visible, showing deep lesions in both parametral areas, with a fundal suture in place and a rectal probe inserted.

The surgeon closes the posterior urinary wall to achieve hemostasis. The next step involves resecting deep lesions from the peritoneal side, where the ovary is attached. The peritoneum, which contains deep lesions, is in close proximity to the ureter and urinary artery. Careful dissection of the ureters on each side is performed to prevent damage, as they are often pulled toward the area of deep lesions.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the ureter, ensuring its direction is under direct observation. The dissection proceeds under the peritoneum to assess the depth of the lesion, avoiding excessive removal of tissue containing nerve fibers. The hypogastric nerve is not dissected, and the deep lesion is isolated for removal. Fatty tissue is visible, indicating clear margins.

The surgeon uses a rectal probe to examine the pelvic cavity, identifying a large peritoneal defect on the right side. The ureter is exposed and carefully dissected, avoiding the hypogastric nerve and its branches. The perimetrium is pulled from the pelvic floor, revealing a cystic lesion approximately three-fourths of a centimeter in size, indicative of endometriotic cysts. The surgeon removes the cyst and the deep lesion from the paramecium on the left side, resulting in a clean pelvic cavity.

The surgeon closes the posterior cul-de-sac and peritonealizes the area to eliminate dead space, preventing small bowel adhesion and obstruction. The ovaries are opened, revealing a peritoneal defect in the anterior cul-de-sac on the right side. A cystic lesion is identified and resected with clear margins, assisted by the surgical team. The defective peritoneum is closed with a pursing suture, and the surface lesion is removed. Specimens are sent to pathology for confirmation of endometriosis.
